formulas.name: Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics
formulas.formula: If two systems are in thermal equilibrium with a third system, then they are also in thermal equilibrium with each other.
formulas.notes: This law allows us to define a temperature scale.

formulas.name: First Law of Thermodynamics
formulas.notes: This law states that energy cannot be created or destroyed, only converted from one form to another.

formulas.name: First Law of Thermodynamics (Internal Energy Change)
formulas.notes: This is an alternative way of expressing the first law.

formulas.name: First Law of Thermodynamics (Work Done)
formulas.notes: This formula is used when the work done is done against a constant external pressure.

formulas.name: First Law of Thermodynamics (Heat Added)

variables.\\frac{dU}{dt}: Rate of change of internal energy
variables.\\frac{dW}{dt}: Rate of change of work done

formulas.notes: This formula is used when the heat added and work done are functions of time.

formulas.name: Second Law of Thermodynamics
formulas.notes: This law states that the total entropy of an isolated system will always increase over time.

formulas.name: Second Law of Thermodynamics (Entropy Change)
formulas.notes: This is an alternative way of expressing the second law.

formulas.name: Third Law of Thermodynamics
formulas.notes: This law states that at absolute zero temperature, the entropy of a system is zero.

formulas.name: Equation of State for an Ideal Gas
formulas.units: Pascals (Pa), Meters cubed (m³), Moles (mol), Joules per Kelvin (J/K), Kelvin (K)
formulas.notes: This equation relates the pressure, volume, and temperature of an ideal gas.

formulas.name: Ideal Gas Constant
formulas.notes: This is the value of the gas constant at standard temperature and pressure.
